bibliographic.citation: Thomas Wright, Anglo-Saxon and Old English Vocabularies, 2nd ed., edited and collated by Richard Paul Wülcker, vol. 1: Vocabularies (London: Trübner & Co., 1884), cols. 536–814.
bibliographic.title: Anglo-Saxon and Old English Vocabularies

entities.description: Wülcker's revised and newly collated second edition of Wright's collection. CME00034 selects vocabularies XIII–XX, printed in columns 536–814, plus the edition title page.
entities.label: Wright and Wülcker, Anglo-Saxon and Old English Vocabularies, second edition, volume I (1884)
notes: The volume prints numbered columns rather than conventional page numbers. Vocabularies XIII–XX occupy capture leaves 302–446 in the complete Internet Archive scan inspected here.

bibliographic.citation: Thomas Wright, A Volume of Vocabularies: Illustrating the Condition and Manners of Our Forefathers, as Well as the History of the Forms of Elementary Education, and of the Languages Spoken in This Island from the Tenth Century to the Fifteenth (Liverpool, 1857).
bibliographic.title: A Volume of Vocabularies

entities.description: Wright's first collection, retained and revised by Wülcker for seven of the eight selected numbered vocabularies. Wülcker's no. XV was a new addition to the second edition.
entities.label: Thomas Wright, A Volume of Vocabularies (1857)

entities.description: A fifteenth-century grammatical and lexical codex. Its dictionary at modern fols. 96r–275r supplied vocabulary XV through a transcript by W. Aldis Wright.

next_steps: Ask Worcester Cathedral Library to reconcile Wülcker's text with current foliation.
Request all surviving leaves and complete binding/object views under the Cathedral's terms.
openQuestions.question: Which current folios of Worcester Cathedral Library F.174 correspond exactly to vocabulary XIII, and can a complete object-level facsimile be obtained?

next_steps: Ask the British Library for the foliation history or inspect a complete reproduction with both modern and historical numeration visible.
openQuestions.question: How does Wülcker's historical locator Harley 978 f. 24v map to the current catalog's ff. 26r–27v for the herb glossary?

next_steps: Submit scoped BL reproduction enquiries for every leaf and binding surface of each object.
Record the supplied files, exact physical boundaries, fees, and reuse terms if obtained.

next_steps: Request all folios and binding surfaces through Beinecke reproduction services.

openQuestions.question: Does Beinecke hold an unpublished complete digital sequence for MS 594, including binding and f. 23, that can be supplied for research?

next_steps: Check current specialist editions or linguistic studies component by component, keeping text composition, witness copying, dialect, and provenance separate.
openQuestions.question: Which historical editorial dates and dialect regions for the eight vocabularies remain accepted in current lexical scholarship?

reviewNotes: The U-M title's phrase 'Anglo-Saxon and Old English' describes the historical collection. The selected medieval vernacular matter is chiefly Middle English; Old English is not assigned merely from that title or its broad subject headings.
Printed locators are numbered columns, while Internet Archive and IIIF locators describe capture leaves or canvases. Those systems are kept separate.

Complete facsimile coverage means the complete sequence exposed by that provider at review time, including covers, inside covers, flyleaves, and blanks when exposed; it does not imply separate spine or edge photography or permission for unrestricted reuse.
The source XML's TEMPHEAD value '8-4-16' is retained as an inherited review notation, not normalized into a bibliographic date because its date order is unstated.

lineage.summary: CME00034 is a University of Michigan electronic selection of vocabularies XIII–XX from volume I of Richard Paul Wülcker's revised 1884 edition of Thomas Wright's Anglo-Saxon and Old English Vocabularies. It is not one medieval work and not the complete printed volume. The selection brings together eight separately transmitted Latin–vernacular lexical texts from seven manuscripts, including two neighboring verse vocabularies in Harley MS 1002. This record distinguishes the repository XML, U-M encoding, Wright's 1857 collection, Wülcker's 1884 revised edition, manuscript witnesses, and digital facsimiles. Complete provider objects are cached where public terms permit; selected-only and request-only coverage is recorded explicitly.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous medieval vocabulary compilers
catalogSummary.date: Components transmitted from the early thirteenth through the fifteenth/early sixteenth century; selected second-edition compilation published 1884
catalogSummary.region: England; component witnesses and dialect associations include Worcester, Reading, Shropshire, Lincolnshire, and tentative western-border and northern areas
catalogSummary.title: Anglo-Saxon and Old English Vocabularies: selected vocabularies XIII–XX

catalogingSubject.label: Anglo-Saxon and Old English Vocabularies: selected vocabularies XIII–XX
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The edition-level selection represented by University of Michigan CME item CME00034: the 1884 title page and eight numbered vocabularies, XIII–XX, at printed cols. 536–814 of Wright and Wülcker's volume I. The unit is neither one medieval composition nor the complete 1884 volume. Its components derive from seven manuscripts and include lexical prose/list structures, two metrical vocabularies, embedded verse, and illustrations.

contentParts.label: XIII. A Semi-Saxon Vocabulary
notes: The historical label Semi-Saxon is retained as an edition title, not as a current language code.
contentParts.summary: A fragmentary early Middle English abridgement/adaptation of the Ælfrician glossary tradition, surviving in binding leaves now Worcester Cathedral Library F.174.

contentParts.label: XIV. A Vocabulary of the Names of Plants
notes: Current and historical foliation have not yet been reconciled.
contentParts.summary: A trilingual Latin, Anglo-Norman, and Middle English list of herbs, likely made for practical or medical use.

contentParts.label: XV. A Latin and English Vocabulary
notes: The manuscript span contains unselected Latin matter; it is not a one-to-one continuous transcript of every entry.
Trinity warns that modern foliation is incorrect but adopts it for reference.
contentParts.summary: English equivalents selected from a much larger Latin dictionary, with an encoded additions section; printed from a transcript supplied by W. Aldis Wright.

contentParts.label: XVI. Metrical Vocabularies
notes: The subscribed name Spencer may identify a copyist; authorship is not assigned.
contentParts.summary: Two Latin metrical school vocabularies, the Distigius and Bursa Latini, with Middle English glosses.

contentParts.label: XVII. A Vocabulary of the Names of the Parts of the Human Body
contentParts.summary: A short Latin metrical list of body-part names with Middle English glosses, beginning Os facies mentum.

contentParts.label: XVIII. An English Vocabulary
notes: The current BL catalog's edition cross-reference starts at p./col. 635, while direct inspection shows XVIII begins at col. 633; the direct edition boundary is preferred.
contentParts.summary: A large Latin–Middle English nominalia beginning with the parts of the human body and extending through themed lexical fields.

contentParts.label: XIX. A Nominale
notes: The edition records a missing/torn leaf; the textual lacuna is not silently reconstructed.
contentParts.summary: The Mayer Nominale, a large Latin–Middle English topical vocabulary in seven encoded chapters, transmitted in a manuscript formerly owned by Joseph Mayer.

contentParts.label: XX. A Pictorial Vocabulary
notes: Beinecke f. 23 contains adjacent notes and letter formulae outside the work portion.
contentParts.summary: A large Latin–Middle English vocabulary accompanied in its manuscript by numerous black and red marginal drawings; the encoding preserves sixty-one FIGURE markers.

assertion.notes: Date of Wülcker's revised selection and numbering, not a shared medieval composition date.
Historical linguistic estimate for the abridged/adapted text; the surviving manuscript is currently dated s. xiii1 and must not be backdated to this estimate.

dateStatements.display: About the middle of the twelfth century according to Wright and Wülcker
First half of the thirteenth century

assertion.notes: The estimate uses the political context of the manuscript; the current parent catalog more broadly dates the composite third quarter of the thirteenth century, possibly 1261–1265.

dateStatements.display: Probably 1264–1265 according to Wright and Wülcker

assertion.notes: Date of the witness, not a precise date for every lexical stratum or later addition.
Current date of the composite witness. Wright and Wülcker thought the texts older, but no secure composition interval is imposed here.
The current specialist record gives s. XV; Wright's narrower 'latter part' estimate is retained only as a historical editorial claim.

extent.description: Eight numbered DIV2 vocabulary components nested in a collection-level DIV1, plus a separate title-page DIV1. The selected text contains 9,548 ITEM elements; one additional ITEM belongs to administrative TEMPHEAD revision metadata. XML computation also finds 280 PB elements, 1,006 MILESTONE elements, 595 NOTE elements, 376 L elements, two LG elements, and sixty-one FIGURE elements. The prose/verse/mixed counts describe encoding form, not eight modern independent works.

formStatements.description: Prose/list vocabularies, two metrical vocabularies, and embedded verse and illustrations in later components
Lexical entries arranged as prose/list structures
Latin metrical vocabularies with English glossing
Predominantly lexical lists with encoded verse lines; XX also includes sixty-one encoded figures

assertion.notes: Includes an early Middle English component in XIII; the collection title and U-M subject headings do not by themselves establish Old English as an intrinsic language of this selection.

scope.description: Headwords, verses, rubrics, and other lexical matter throughout the eight bilingual/trilingual components
French plant-name equivalents in the trilingual herb list
Nineteenth-century titles, introductions, explanations, and notes

notes: Thomas Wright and Richard Wülcker are cataloged as editors/compilers of the nineteenth-century selection, not as medieval authors.
Text composition, witness copying, institutional provenance, dialect, printed edition, U-M encoding, repository XML, and digital facsimile are modeled as distinct layers.
The preferred title adds 'selected vocabularies XIII–XX' because the inherited U-M title alone could imply complete coverage of the 1884 volume.
The title-page phrase '[All rights reserved.]' is a historical 1884 publication statement and does not override the current U.S. public-domain rights evidence modeled in the lineage manifest.

next_steps: Request a current folio concordance from Worcester Cathedral Library and compare it with the 1884 edition.

next_steps: Ask the British Library for foliation history or inspect a complete reproduction with both numerations visible.

next_steps: Compare the forms cited by Wright and Wülcker with current Linguistic Atlas and specialist vocabulary scholarship.

assertion.notes: Wülcker retained Wright's explanations, marked his own additions R.W., removed five first-edition articles, and added three new ones, including no. XV.

scope.description: Transcript of the dictionary at Trinity O.5.4, modern ff. 96r–275r

summaries.value: An edition-level selection of eight anonymous medieval lexical texts, numbered XIII–XX in Richard Paul Wülcker's revised 1884 edition of Thomas Wright's vocabulary collection. The components range from an early Middle English abridgement of an Ælfrician glossary and a trilingual herb list to schoolroom metrical vocabularies, large nominalia, and an illustrated Latin–English word list. Together they document language teaching, material culture, lexical organization, dialect, and nineteenth-century manuscript editing.

The eight components are XIII A Semi-Saxon Vocabulary; XIV A Vocabulary of the Names of Plants; XV A Latin and English Vocabulary; XVI Metrical Vocabularies; XVII A Vocabulary of the Names of the Parts of the Human Body; XVIII An English Vocabulary; XIX A Nominale; and XX A Pictorial Vocabulary. XV includes an additions section, and XIX contains seven internal chapters.

CME00034 preserves the 1884 edition title page and the continuous printed run from col. 536 through col. 814, corresponding to complete scan leaves 302–446. It omits vocabularies I–XII, the edition's other preliminaries and index matter, and any volume beyond this selected run.

assertion.notes: The supplied qualifier prevents the U-M item from being mistaken for the complete printed volume.

titles.value: Anglo-Saxon and Old English Vocabularies: selected vocabularies XIII–XX

assertion.notes: This title belongs to the complete edition; CME00034 contains only nos. XIII–XX and the title page.

work.summary: An edition-level selection of eight anonymous medieval lexical texts, numbered XIII–XX in Richard Paul Wülcker's revised 1884 edition of Thomas Wright's vocabulary collection. The components range from an early Middle English abridgement of an Ælfrician glossary and a trilingual herb list to schoolroom metrical vocabularies, large nominalia, and an illustrated Latin–English word list. Together they document language teaching, material culture, lexical organization, dialect, and nineteenth-century manuscript editing.
